era_context: The late 6th century BCE, with the Neo-Babylonian Empire under Nebuchadnezzar II at its height, the Achaemenid Persian Empire consolidating control over the eastern Mediterranean and Near East, the Spring and Autumn period in China with increasing interstate conflict and philosophical development, and the early Classical period in Greece. This is an era of imperial consolidation, religious transformation, and the development of philosophical and religious traditions that would define the next millennium.

events.title: Battle of the Eclipse (Halys River)
events.region: Anatolia, border between Lydia and Media
events.description: According to the Greek historian Herodotus, a solar eclipse occurred during a battle between the Lydian forces under King Alyattes and the Median forces under King Cyaxares on the Halys River (modern Kızılırmak River in Anatolia). The eclipse so frightened both armies that they ceased fighting and agreed to a peace treaty, mediated by the Cilician king Syennesis. This event is famous in the history of astronomy because Herodotus's account can be used to date the eclipse: a total solar eclipse visible from Anatolia occurred on May 28, 585 BCE. This eclipse is one of the earliest astronomical events that can be precisely dated using modern astronomical calculations. However, the historical significance of the battle itself is debated; some scholars question whether the eclipse actually caused the cessation of fighting or whether Herodotus embellished the account.
events.certainty_note: The solar eclipse of May 28, 585 BCE is confirmed by modern astronomical calculations. Herodotus's account of the battle and the eclipse is the primary historical source, but it was written centuries after the event (Herodotus lived c. 484–425 BCE). The eclipse itself is confirmed; the historical significance of the battle and whether the eclipse actually caused the cessation of fighting are debated. The date 585 BCE is secure for the eclipse; the historical narrative around it is less certain.
cross_references: Related to broader Lydian-Median conflicts in Anatolia
Marks a significant moment in early Greek historiography

events.title: Thales of Miletus predicts the solar eclipse
events.description: According to later Greek sources, the philosopher and mathematician Thales of Miletus (c. 624–546 BCE) predicted the solar eclipse of 585 BCE. This prediction is celebrated as one of the earliest examples of scientific prediction in Western history, demonstrating the application of mathematical and astronomical knowledge to forecast natural phenomena. However, modern scholars debate whether Thales actually made a precise prediction of the eclipse or whether later sources exaggerated his achievement. Some scholars suggest that Thales may have predicted an eclipse within a general time period rather than specifying the exact date and location. Regardless of the precise nature of his prediction, Thales is recognized as a pioneering figure in Greek philosophy and natural science, and the eclipse of 585 BCE became associated with his name in later tradition.

sources.name: Diogenes Laërtius, Lives of Eminent Philosophers

events.certainty_note: The attribution of the eclipse prediction to Thales is traditional, based on later Greek sources (Herodotus, Diogenes Laërtius, Pliny). Thales himself left no written works, and the accounts of his prediction vary in detail. Modern scholars debate whether Thales made a precise prediction or whether later sources exaggerated his achievement. The eclipse itself is confirmed by astronomical calculations; the attribution of the prediction to Thales is less certain.
cross_references: Part of broader development of Greek natural philosophy

events.title: Continued Babylonian consolidation under Nebuchadnezzar II
events.description: In 585 BCE, Nebuchadnezzar II continued his consolidation of Babylonian power and the construction of major building projects in Babylon. The reign of Nebuchadnezzar II (605–562 BCE) was marked by extensive military campaigns, the expansion of Babylonian territory, and the construction of monumental architecture, including the famous Hanging Gardens (though the historical existence and location of the Hanging Gardens remain debated). Nebuchadnezzar's reign represented the height of Neo-Babylonian power and influence. In 585 BCE, he was approximately 20 years into his reign and had already established Babylonian hegemony over much of the Near East. His military campaigns and building projects made Babylon one of the most powerful and culturally significant cities in the ancient world.
key_figures: Nebuchadnezzar II (King of Babylon, r. 605–562 BCE)
events.certainty_note: Nebuchadnezzar II's reign and his consolidation of Babylonian power are confirmed by the Babylonian Chronicles and archaeological evidence. The specific events of 585 BCE are less clearly documented, but the broader pattern of his reign is well-established. The Hanging Gardens are traditionally attributed to Nebuchadnezzar, but their historical existence and location are debated by modern scholars.
cross_references: Part of broader Neo-Babylonian imperial consolidation

events.title: Confucius continues teaching and seeking political patronage
events.region: China, State of Lu and surrounding territories
events.description: Confucius (551–479 BCE), now approximately 34 years old, continued his role as a teacher and philosopher during the Spring and Autumn period. He was engaged in transmitting traditional ritual knowledge and ethical teachings to his disciples while seeking political patronage among the various warring states. This period marks the consolidation of Confucian thought, emphasizing virtue (de), proper conduct, and the importance of ritual propriety (li) in governance and social relations. Though Confucius himself did not achieve major political power during his lifetime, his teachings were beginning to attract followers who would spread his influence throughout China and eventually East Asia.

sources.name: Shiji (Records of the Grand Historian) by Sima Qian

events.certainty_note: Confucius's life dates (551–479 BCE) are traditionally accepted, making him approximately 34 years old in 585 BCE. However, specific events of individual years are not documented in contemporary sources. The Analects and Shiji are later compilations and do not provide year-by-year chronology. His activity as a teacher during this period is certain, but specific events of 585 BCE are not documented.
cross_references: Part of broader Spring and Autumn period intellectual development

disconfirming_evidence: The Battle of the Halys and the role of the eclipse in causing the cessation of fighting are debated by modern scholars. While the eclipse itself is confirmed by astronomical calculations, the historical narrative around it (that the eclipse frightened both armies into ceasing fighting) is questioned. Some scholars suggest that Herodotus embellished the account or that the eclipse was not the primary cause of the peace treaty. The prediction of the eclipse by Thales is traditional but uncertain; modern scholars debate whether Thales made a precise prediction or whether later sources exaggerated his achievement. The existence and location of the Hanging Gardens of Babylon, traditionally attributed to Nebuchadnezzar II, are debated by modern scholars; some scholars question whether they ever existed or whether they were located in Babylon at all.
historiographic_note: The historical record for 585 BCE is moderate in richness. The solar eclipse is confirmed by astronomical calculations and is one of the earliest astronomical events that can be precisely dated. Herodotus's account of the Battle of the Halys is the primary historical source, but it was written centuries after the event and may be embellished. The Babylonian Chronicles provide documentation of Nebuchadnezzar II's reign, though specific events of 585 BCE are not clearly dated. Chinese sources (Shiji, Analects) are later compilations but represent the best available documentation for Confucius. The record is dominated by Near Eastern and Greek sources; Chinese sources are less frequently cited in Western historiography. Sub-Saharan Africa, Mesoamerica, Northern Europe, and Southeast Asia remain essentially absent from the historical record. The historiography of the eclipse and the Battle of the Halys is influenced by the later Greek tradition, which may have emphasized the role of the eclipse in causing the peace treaty.

graph_edges.note: The solar eclipse of 585 BCE is associated with Thales's prediction
Thales's prediction is associated with the solar eclipse of 585 BCE
Nebuchadnezzar II's consolidation of power continued after the fall of Jerusalem
Confucius's teaching during this period established the philosophical tradition that dominated East Asia
